[421]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 逮 - Means 'when' or 'upon,' often used to indicate the time of an event.
  • 又遣使贡奉 - Means 'sent envoys to offer tribute,' indicating diplomatic or ceremonial actions.
  • 恳请玉册金劵 - Means 'earnestly requested the imperial jade tablet and golden scroll,' referring to symbols of imperial authority.
  • 有司定议 - Means 'the officials decided,' indicating official deliberation.
  • 非天子不得用 - Means 'only the emperor could use them,' emphasizing the exclusivity of imperial symbols.
  • 后竟赐之 - Means 'later, they were granted to him,' indicating a change in status.
  • 即以节钺授其子元瓘 - Means 'immediately gave the staff and axe to his son Yuan Guan,' referring to symbols of military authority.
  • 自称吴越国王 - Means 'proclaimed himself King of Wu and Yue,' indicating a claim to royal status.
  • 名其居曰殿 - Means 'named his residence 'Palace,'' indicating a grandiose self-designation.
  • 官属悉称臣 - Means 'all officials called him 'Your Excellency,' indicating a shift in the form of address.
  • 于衣锦军大建玉册金券诏书三楼 - Means 'built three-story buildings for the imperial jade tablet, golden scroll, and imperial edicts in the Army of Embroidered Robes,' indicating grandiose construction.
  • 遣使册东夷诸国 - Means 'sent envoys to confer titles on the Eastern Barbarian states,' indicating diplomatic actions.
  • 防极其势 - Means 'exploited his power to the fullest,' indicating the extent of his influence.
  • 与向之谢表所陈处极防防累恩贾祸之诫殊相戾矣 - Means 'contradicted the warnings in his earlier memorial about the dangers of accumulating favors and inviting disasters,' indicating a shift in behavior.
  • 遂飘然入蜀 - Means 'then he flew away to Shu,' indicating a departure.
  • 后果为安重诲奏削王爵以太师致仕 - Means 'later, he was stripped of his royal title by An Zhonghe and retired as a Grand Tutor,' indicating a change in status.
  • 重诲死明宗乃复镠爵位 - Means 'after An Zhonghe's death, Emperor Mingzong restored Li Yu's royal title,' indicating a restoration of status.
  • 武肃之逾越固莫逃乎二书所论 - Means 'Li Baoju's transgressions could not escape the criticism in these two books,' indicating a reference to historical criticism.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 庄宗 - Refers to Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang, a ruler of the Later Tang dynasty.
  • 吴越 - Refers to the Kingdom of Wuyue, a regional state during the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period.
  • 玉册金劵 - Imperial symbols of authority, often used in coronations or significant ceremonies.
  • 节钺 - Symbols of military authority, often given to high-ranking officials or princes.
  • 禅月贯休 - A famous Tang dynasty poet and Buddhist monk.
  • 安重诲 - A powerful eunuch and regent during the Later Tang dynasty.
  • 明宗 - Refers to Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang, the ruler who succeeded Zhuangzong.
  • 武肃 - Refers to Li Baoju (also known as Wang Shenzhi), the founder of the Kingdom of Wuyue.
  • 二书 - Refers to historical texts or records that criticize Li Baoju's actions.

Proper Nouns

  • 庄宗 - Emperor Zhuangzong of Later Tang.
  • 吴越 - Kingdom of Wuyue.
  • 玉册金劵 - Imperial symbols of authority.
  • 节钺 - Symbols of military authority.
  • 元瓘 - Son of Li Yu, who received the staff and axe.
  • 禅月贯休 - Famous Tang dynasty poet and Buddhist monk.
  • 安重诲 - Powerful eunuch and regent during the Later Tang dynasty.
  • 明宗 - Emperor Mingzong of Later Tang.
  • 武肃 - Li Baoju (also known as Wang Shenzhi), founder of the Kingdom of Wuyue.
  • 二书 - Historical texts or records that criticize Li Baoju's actions.

[422]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 射字法 - A method of learning to read and write Chinese characters through a game-like process involving rhythm and memory.
  • 必湏彼我二人俱聪明熟于翻切优于记问者方乃便防 - A complex phrase meaning 'both parties must be intelligent, proficient in pronunciation, and skilled in memorization to make it effective.'
  • 拊掌 - A gesture of clapping hands, used here as a signal for the other person to respond.
  • 留情于此 - A phrase meaning 'to focus on this,' indicating the act of concentrating on the task at hand.
  • 优游无事 - Means 'leisurely and without worries,' describing a state of relaxed and carefree living.
  • 谩以取一时之笑乐 - Means 'to amuse oneself with laughter for a moment,' indicating a lighthearted and temporary activity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 射字法 - A traditional educational method used in ancient China to teach reading and writing, often involving rhythmic clapping and memory games.
  • 翻切 - A phonetic system used in Classical Chinese to represent sounds, similar to modern pinyin.
  • 鼙鼓之声 - Refers to the sound of war drums, symbolizing military action.
  • 干戈之锋 - Refers to the sharp edges of weapons, symbolizing conflict and warfare.
  • 优游无事 - A phrase reflecting the ideal of a peaceful and harmonious life, often associated with Confucian ideals of governance and personal conduct.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long, compound sentence '必湏彼我二人俱聪明熟于翻切优于记问者方乃便防' must be read as a single condition for the effectiveness of the method.
  • The sentence '倘遇人以诗词或言语示我彼在隔坐不及知闻我则拊掌彼便说出与所示同然片段文章皆可成诵非特一句一字而已' describes the process of the game, where one person claps to signal the other to repeat what they hear.
  • The clause '用拊掌代击鼓殊无勾肆市井俗态' explains the difference between the game and a more formal or militaristic setting.
  • The clause '此天下太平优游无事谩以取一时之笑乐耳' provides the context and purpose of the game, emphasizing its lighthearted nature.
  • The rhetorical question '使鼙鼓之声震天干戈之锋耀日又能留情于此耶' contrasts the game with the seriousness of war.

[423]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 晴涎】丁【丁颠】檠【檠防】盈【盈延】能【能○】称【称千】非○○精【精煎】零【零连】汀【汀天】橙【橙纒】东钟江支兹为防鱼胡模齐乖佳灰咍真谆臻匡亏元魂痕寒欢关山先森萧宵爻豪歌戈麻阳唐耕斜荣青蒸登尤侯车侵潭谭盐添横光凡如欲切春字清谆清清千春清字在第三行第一字谆字在第七行第四字拊掌则前三后一少歇又前七后四夏字平声为霞盈麻盈盈延霞盈字在第三行第七字麻字在第十行第二字拊掌则前三后七少歇又前十后二少歇又三盖夏字去声所以又三也若入声则四矣余仿此但字母不离二十八字而叶韵莫逃五十六字此为至要后见賔退 - A complex and highly stylized Classical Chinese passage, possibly a mnemonic or a phonetic guide. It contains a mix of characters and symbols that are not standard, making it challenging to interpret directly.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 賔退 - Could refer to the act of a guest or visitor departing, which might be part of a larger context or ritual.

Proper Nouns

  • 賔退 - Could refer to the act of a guest or visitor departing, which might be part of a larger context or ritual.

[424]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 録一则与此略同并志之 - Record one example similar to this and record it together.
  • 俗间有击鼓射字之伎 - In the common folk, there is a skill of hitting drums and shooting characters.
  • 莫知所始 - No one knows its origin.
  • 盖全用切字该以两诗 - It is entirely composed of phonetic characters and includes two poems.
  • 诗皆七言一篇六句四十二字 - Both poems are seven-character lines, one has six lines and forty-two characters.
  • 以代三十六字母 - To represent the thirty-six phonetic symbols.
  • 全用五支至十二齐韵 - It uses the five zhi to twelve qi rhymes.
  • 取其声相近便于诵习 - To choose sounds that are similar to facilitate recitation and learning.
  • 一篇七句四十九字以该平声五十七韵 - One poem has seven lines and forty-nine characters to cover the fifty-seven level tones.
  • 如一字字母在第三句第四字则鼓节前三后四叶韵亦如之 - If a phonetic symbol is in the fourth character of the third line, then the drum beats should be three before and four after, and the rhyme should be similar.
  • 又以一二三四为平上去入之别 - Use one, two, three, four to distinguish the level, rising, departing, and entering tones.
  • 诗曰 - The poem says.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 击鼓射字 - A traditional Chinese game where players hit drums and shoot arrows at characters.
  • 三十六字母 - The thirty-six phonetic symbols used in the Chinese language, standardized during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 五支至十二齐韵 - Specific rhyme scheme used in the poems, part of the Chinese poetic tradition.
  • 平上去入之别 - Four tones in Middle Chinese: level, rising, departing, and entering.

Proper Nouns

  • 三十六字母 - Thirty-six phonetic symbols used in the Chinese language.
  • 五支至十二齐韵 - Specific rhyme scheme used in the poems.
  • 平上去入之别 - Four tones in Middle Chinese: level, rising, departing, and entering.

[425]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 古累累然 - Describes ancient tombs or ruins as numerous and scattered, creating a sense of historical depth and richness.
  • 左手握无底圎桶右手臂鹰人之脑心为窍以安吸子 - Describes the person holding a bottomless round bucket in their left hand and using an eagle's brain and heart as a hole to hold a suction cup.
  • 吸子顶防大正盖脑心俨一席防象人衣褶 - Describes the top of the suction cup as being large and perfectly shaped, resembling the brain and heart of an eagle, and the folds of the person's clothing.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 松江 - A region in eastern China, now part of Shanghai, known for its historical significance and natural beauty.
  • 横云山 - A mountain range in Songjiang, known for its scenic views and historical sites.
  • 晋陆氏 - Refers to the Lu family during the Jin dynasty, a period known for its cultural and political developments.
  • 太元二年 - The second year of the Taoyuan era (377 AD) under Emperor Xiaowu of Eastern Jin, a time of significant cultural and literary activity.
  • 水滴 - A small container used for watering plants, often made of bronze or other materials, reflecting the practical and artistic aspects of daily life in ancient China.

Proper Nouns

  • 松江 - A region in eastern China.
  • 横云山 - A mountain range in Songjiang.
  • 晋陆氏 - The Lu family during the Jin dynasty.
  • 太元二年 - The second year of the Taoyuan era (377 AD).
  • 水滴 - A small container used for watering plants.

[426]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 及 - Classical connective meaning 'when' or 'at the time of.'
  • 通身青緑 - Describes the overall color of the object as a deep green.
  • 吸子 - Refers to a specific part of the object, possibly a figurine or a component.
  • 浑若碧玉 - Means 'as if made of emerald jade,' emphasizing the object's appearance.
  • 论其制作肤理 - Discusses the craftsmanship and texture of the object.
  • 寳惜 - Means 'to treasure' or 'to cherish.'
  • 殉葬 - Refers to the practice of burying objects with the deceased.
  • 剔凿沙土饰泽蜡石 - Describes the process of cleaning and polishing the object.
  • 神气百倍于昔 - Means 'the spirit and vitality are a hundred times greater than before.'
  • 韫椟寳藏 - Means 'to store in a chest and treasure.'
  • 示博古好雅者 - Means 'to show to those who appreciate ancient artifacts and elegance.'
  • 夺去 - Means 'to take away forcibly.'
  • 寓杭州防桥 - Refers to living in Hangzhou at Fang Bridge.
  • 临湖有水阁 - Describes a waterside pavilion.
  • 倚阑把玩 - Means 'to lean against the railing and admire.'
  • 堕吸子于湖水中 - Means 'the figurine fell into the lake.'
  • 悒怏嘅叹 - Means 'to sigh and lament.'
  • 形神为之凋枯 - Means 'the body and spirit withered.'
  • 他徃 - Means 'to go elsewhere.'
  • 逾三年 - Means 'more than three years.'
  • 仍居昔所寓舎 - Means 'still lived in the former residence.'
  • 追懐故物 - Means 'to recall the old object.'
  • 霜降水静之时 - Describes a time when the water is calm after a frost.
  • 易号曰神人狮子 - Means 'renamed it as the Divine Lion.'
  • 序述颠末 - Means 'to narrate the beginning and end.'
  • 歌咏以张之 - Means 'to praise and celebrate it.'
  • 巨轴 - Refers to a large scroll.
  • 公殁 - Means 'the gentleman passed away.'
  • 子孙不能世守 - Means 'the descendants could not preserve it for generations.'
  • 善贾而沽诸 - Means 'to sell it to a good merchant.'
  • 自我朝百余年来 - Means 'since the beginning of our dynasty for over a hundred years.'
  • 可以屈指数也 - Means 'can be counted on one's fingers.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 晋人 - People from the Jin dynasty (266-420 CE).
  • 汉器 - Objects from the Han dynasty (206 BCE - 220 CE).
  • 殉葬 - The practice of burying objects with the deceased, common in ancient China.
  • 杭州防桥 - A location in Hangzhou, known for its historical significance.
  • 神人狮子 - A title given to the object, suggesting it is considered divine or sacred.
  • 馆阁诸老 - Refers to respected scholars or officials.
  • 骚人雅士 - Refers to poets and literati.

Proper Nouns

  • 鲜于困学公 - A historical figure named Xian Yu Kunnxue.
  • 士安 - Refers to the narrator or the person who owns the object.
  • 杭州 - The city of Hangzhou.
  • 防桥 - A specific location in Hangzhou.
  • 婺州 - The city of Wuzhou.
  • 陶氏 - A family named Tao in Wuzhou.
  • 神人狮子 - A title given to the object.
  • 巨轴 - A large scroll.

[427]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 至元钞様 - A model or prototype of the '至元钞' currency issued during the Yuan Dynasty.
  • 中书左丞 - An official title in the Yuan Dynasty, equivalent to a high-ranking minister.
  • 泥金饰斋扁 - A gold-leaf-decorated plaque for a study room.
  • 笔里无时那解有命中有处未应无 - A poetic phrase about the inevitability of fate and destiny.
  • 百千万世传名节 - To be remembered for one's integrity through countless generations.
  • 二十三年非故居 - Twenty-three years is not a long time in one's life.
  • 寄语长安朱紫客 - Addressing the officials and nobles in the capital.
  • 尽心好上帝王书 - To serve the emperor with all one's heart.
  • 遂遭黥流岭南 - He was branded and exiled to the southern regions.
  • 及恩放还 - And was later pardoned and allowed to return.
  • 与似道遇诸途 - And met Jia Sidao on the road.
  • 君来路吾归路来来去去何时住 - Your path is my path; coming and going, when will it stop?
  • 公田关子竟何如 - What has become of the public land and tax system?
  • 国事当时谁汝误 - Who was responsible for the state affairs at that time?
  • 人生防有相逢处 - One should always be prepared to meet others.
  • 客中颇恨乏蒸羊 - In exile, he greatly missed the delicacy of steamed lamb.
  • 聊赠一篇长短句 - I will casually offer a short poem.
  • 归附后入京上书言时相并献至元钞様 - After surrendering, he entered the capital and submitted a memorial, proposing the use of the '至元钞' model.
  • 此様在宋时固尝进呈请以代关子朝廷不能用故今别改年号而复献之 - This model was previously presented during the Song Dynasty, requesting its use to replace the '关子,' but the court did not adopt it, so he presented it again after changing the era name.
  • 世皇嘉纳使用铸板以功累官至今任而终 - The emperor praised and adopted it, using it for casting coins, and he was promoted through his contributions, serving until the end of his life.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 至元钞 - A type of paper money issued during the Yuan Dynasty, named after the era name '至元' (Zhiyuan).
  • 叶公亦愚 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official, whose life and works are not widely documented.
  • 贾似道 - A powerful chancellor during the late Southern Song Dynasty, known for his controversial policies and eventual downfall.
  • 公田关子 - A financial instrument or land tax system associated with Jia Sidao's policies, which were criticized for their harshness.
  • 黥流岭南 - A form of punishment involving branding and exile to the southern regions, a common penalty for political dissidents.
  • 世皇 - Refers to Kublai Khan, the founder of the Yuan Dynasty, who is often referred to as '世皇' (Shihuang) in historical texts.
  • 铸板 - Refers to the process of casting coins or metal plates for currency.

Proper Nouns

  • 叶公亦愚 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official, whose life and works are not widely documented.
  • 贾似道 - A prominent political figure during the late Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 至元钞 - A type of paper money issued during the Yuan Dynasty.
  • 世皇 - Refers to Kublai Khan, the founder of the Yuan Dynasty.

[428]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 白眉 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the white eyebrow,' referring to someone who is outstanding or the best in a group.
  • 嘲风咏月惜玉怜香 - Collective phrase describing the refined and poetic pursuits of literati: 'to mock the wind,' 'to sing about the moon,' 'to cherish jade,' and 'to pity fragrant things.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 歌妓顺时秀 - Name of a courtesan, a common figure in Chinese literature and history, often associated with high culture and intellectual circles.
  • 翰林学士王公元鼎 - High-ranking official in the Yuan Dynasty, known for his literary and administrative skills.
  • 马版肠 - Historical practice of using horse intestines in traditional Chinese medicine or cuisine.
  • 燮理隂阳致君泽民 - Confucian ideals of governance and social harmony: 'to harmonize and manage,' 'balance of yin and yang,' 'to assist the ruler,' and 'to benefit the people.'
  • 至正辛丑四月朔日 - Specific date and year (1341 during the Yuan Dynasty) providing historical context for the event.
  • 天官书 - The *Tian Guan Shu* (Celestial Officials Book), a historical text providing cultural and astrological significance.
  • 王晋书 - The *Jin Shu* (Book of Jin), a historical text providing cultural and astrological significance.
  • 京房易传 - The *Yi Chuan* (Commentary on the Book of Changes) by Jing Fang, a significant work in Chinese philosophy and divination.

Proper Nouns

  • 歌妓顺时秀 - Name of the courtesan.
  • 郭氏 - Surname of the courtesan.
  • 翰林学士王公元鼎 - Name and title of the high-ranking official.
  • 马版肠 - Specific type of food or medicinal ingredient.
  • 至正辛丑 - Year 1341 during the Yuan Dynasty.
  • 四月朔日 - First day of the fourth month.
  • 天官书 - The *Tian Guan Shu* (Celestial Officials Book).
  • 王晋书 - The *Jin Shu* (Book of Jin).
  • 京房易传 - The *Yi Chuan* (Commentary on the Book of Changes) by Jing Fang.

[429]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 至正壬寅八月中 - Refers to the eighth month of 1352, a significant year during the Yuan Dynasty.
  • 辰字围 - A specific area or district, possibly named 'Chen'.
  • 已阉雄狗 - A castrated male dog, which was sometimes used in divination practices.
  • 嘴爪红如鲜血然 - Describes a physical anomaly, which was often interpreted as an omen.
  • 犬之为妖 - Dogs were often seen as omens or signs of supernatural events in Chinese culture.
  • 占騐之书 - Books of divination were widely used in ancient China to predict the future and interpret omens.
  • 兵戈乱离 - Refers to periods of war and chaos, which were common in Chinese history.
  • 甲辰四月十五日 - Refers to the 15th day of the fourth month of 1364, a significant date during the transition from the Yuan to the Ming Dynasty.
  • 华亭县五保杨巷邵浦云之西清庵廊屋一十九间 - A specific location, a temple with nineteen rooms, which was a common type of religious structure in ancient China.
  • 乾坤变异録 - A book that records changes in the heavens and earth, which was used to interpret omens and predict future events.
  • 五行志 - A book on the five elements, which is a fundamental concept in Chinese philosophy and cosmology.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 至正壬寅八月中 - Refers to the eighth month of 1352, a significant year during the Yuan Dynasty.
  • 辰字围 - A specific area or district, possibly named 'Chen'.
  • 已阉雄狗 - A castrated male dog, which was sometimes used in divination practices.
  • 嘴爪红如鲜血然 - Describes a physical anomaly, which was often interpreted as an omen.
  • 犬之为妖 - Dogs were often seen as omens or signs of supernatural events in Chinese culture.
  • 占騐之书 - Books of divination were widely used in ancient China to predict the future and interpret omens.
  • 兵戈乱离 - Refers to periods of war and chaos, which were common in Chinese history.
  • 甲辰四月十五日 - Refers to the 15th day of the fourth month of 1364, a significant date during the transition from the Yuan to the Ming Dynasty.
  • 华亭县五保杨巷邵浦云之西清庵廊屋一十九间 - A specific location, a temple with nineteen rooms, which was a common type of religious structure in ancient China.
  • 乾坤变异録 - A book that records changes in the heavens and earth, which was used to interpret omens and predict future events.
  • 五行志 - A book on the five elements, which is a fundamental concept in Chinese philosophy and cosmology.

Proper Nouns

  • 至正 - The era name of Emperor Toghon Temür, the last emperor of the Yuan Dynasty.
  • 壬寅 - A year in the Chinese zodiac, corresponding to the year 1352.
  • 上海县 - A county in the Yuan Dynasty.
  • 三十四保 - A district within the county.
  • 辰字围 - A specific area or district, possibly named 'Chen'.
  • 金寿 - A family name.
  • 甲辰 - A year in the Chinese zodiac, corresponding to the year 1364.
  • 华亭县 - A county in the Yuan Dynasty.
  • 五保 - A district within the county.
  • 杨巷邵浦云之西清庵 - The name of a temple.
  • 乾坤变异録 - A book recording changes in the heavens and earth.
  • 五行志 - A book on the five elements.

[430]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 人之四支百骸 - Refers to the limbs and bones of the human body, a common way to describe the human form in classical texts.
  • 莫不畏寒 - Means 'none fear cold,' a hyperbolic expression indicating extreme sensitivity to cold.
  • 獨面則否 - Means 'but the face is not,' indicating an exception to the general rule.
  • 医书谓 - Means 'medical texts say,' introducing a citation or authoritative statement.
  • 头者诸阳之防 - Means 'the head is the barrier of all yang,' a medical concept referring to the head's role in protecting yang energy.
  • 诸阳脉上至头 - Means 'the yang meridians ascend to the head,' describing the path of yang meridians.
  • 所以然也 - Means 'that is why,' explaining the reason for the preceding statement.
  • 六十甲子之有纳音 - Refers to the sixty cycles of the Chinese calendar and their corresponding musical tones.
  • 世人鲜知其理 - Means 'few people understand its principles,' indicating the rarity of knowledge about the topic.
  • 尝观笔谈 - Means 'I have read *Dream Pool Essays*,' referring to Shen Kuo's work.
  • 六十甲子纳音盖六十律旋相为宫也 - Means 'the musical tones of the sixty cycles are based on the sixty scales rotating to form the tonic,' a complex musical and calendrical concept.
  • 一律合五音十二律纳六十音也 - Means 'one scale combines five tones and twelve scales to form sixty tones,' further explaining the musical structure.
  • 凡气始于东方而右行 - Means 'the qi starts in the east and moves to the right,' describing the direction of qi flow.
  • 音起于西方而左行 - Means 'the tones start in the west and move to the left,' describing the direction of tone flow.
  • 所以气始于东方者 - Means 'the reason qi starts in the east is,' explaining the direction of qi flow.
  • 四时始于木右行传于火火传于土土传于金金传于水 - Means 'the four seasons start with wood, moving to the right to fire, then to earth, then to metal, then to water,' describing the sequence of the five elements.

  • 五音始于金左旋传于火火传于木木传于水水传于土 - Means 'the five tones start with metal, moving to the left to fire, then to wood, then to water, then to earth,' describing the sequence of the five tones.
  • 纳音与易纳甲同法 - Means 'the method of musical tones is the same as the method of *Yi* (I Ching) in *Naji* (Nai Jia),' comparing the two systems.
  • 干纳甲而坤纳癸 - Means 'Qian (Heaven) takes Jia while Kun (Earth) takes Gui,' referring to the hexagrams in the *I Ching*.

  • 避兵云间泗濵 - Means 'to avoid war in the cloud region by the Si River,' indicating a place of refuge.
  • 林泉之胜 - Means 'the beauty of forests and springs,' suggesting a natural, scenic environment.
  • 无烽燧之虞 - Means 'without the fear of beacon fires,' indicating safety from war.
  • 嘉遯者 - Means 'those who retire with honor,' referring to respected recluses.
  • 文人高士 - Means 'scholars and high-ranking gentlemen,' referring to respected intellectuals.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 林昉田间书 - Refers to a book written by Lin Fang, a scholar known for his works on rural life and philosophy.
  • 游山檄 - A formal announcement or proclamation inviting friends to go on a mountain trip.
  • 云间泗濵 - Refers to a specific location, possibly a region near the Si River, known for its natural beauty and safety.
  • 林泉之胜 - A common literary motif referring to the beauty of nature, often used to describe idyllic retreats.
  • 烽燧 - Beacon fires used in ancient China to signal the approach of enemies, indicating a state of war.
  • 嘉遯者 - Refers to respected recluses who choose to withdraw from society, often for moral or political reasons.
  • 文人高士 - Scholars and intellectuals who are highly respected for their moral and intellectual achievements.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 因仿司马温公故事 - Following the example of Master Sima Wen Gong, referring to the practice of creating agreements or rules, likely in a manner similar to those established by the historical figure Sima Guang.
  • 俾予作约语 - "Having me write the agreement," where "俾" means to have or to make someone do something.
  • 四时行乐我辈亦风月主人 - "Enjoying the four seasons, we too are masters of the moon and wind," suggesting that one should enjoy life's pleasures as if they were the hosts of nature.
  • 幸居同泗水之滨况地接九山之胜 - "Fortunately living by the banks of the Si River, and moreover, being near the beauty of the Nine Mountains," indicating a fortunate location.
  • 节序骎骎莫芒鞵竹杖杯盘草草何惭野蔌山肴 - "As the seasons hurry by, do not hesitate to wear straw shoes, carry a bamboo cane, and have simple meals, what is there to be ashamed of?" This encourages simplicity and contentment.
  • 虽云一饷之清懽亦是百年之嘉话 - "Even a moment of clear joy can be a good story for a lifetime," highlighting the value of even small moments of happiness.
  • 敢烦同志互作遨头慨元祐之耆英衣冠逺矣集永和之少长觞咏依然 - "I dare ask my fellow scholars to take turns as host, reflecting on the distinguished scholars of the Yuanyou era whose glory has faded, while gathering the young and old of the Yonghe era who still share in the joy of wine and poetry," a call to continue the tradition of scholarly gatherings.
  • 订约既勤践言弗替用附于此以见真率之防不让逰山之乐也 - "Having made this agreement diligently, we should not neglect to follow through with our words, and thus I record it here to show that our sincerity is no less than the joy of traveling to the mountains," emphasizing the importance of keeping one's promises.
  • 珠帘秀 - "Pearl Curtain Beauty," likely a stage name or nickname for a performer, possibly a courtesan or actress known for her beauty and talent.
  • 姿容姝丽 - "Graceful and beautiful appearance," describing someone who is attractive and elegant.
  • 杂剧当今独歩 - "Unrivaled in today's mixed drama," indicating that the person is the best in her field.
  • 胡紫山宣慰极钟爱之 - "Mr. Hu Zishan, the Pacifier, deeply loves her," where "宣慰" refers to a high-ranking official responsible for maintaining order and peace.
  • 沉醉东风小曲 - "A short song titled 'Chen Zui Dong Feng' (Drunk by the East Wind)," likely a piece of music or poetry.
  • 锦织江边翠竹绒穿海上明珠 - "Silk woven from green bamboo by the river, fine threads embroidered with pearls from the sea," a metaphorical description of the beauty and craftsmanship.
  • 月淡时风清处都隔断落红尘土 - "When the moon is faint and the wind is clear, they all separate from the fallen red dust," a poetic way of saying that beauty and clarity can transcend worldly concerns.
  • 一片闲情任卷舒挂尽朝云暮雨 - "A piece of idle sentiment, freely rolling and unrolling, hanging up morning clouds and evening rain," suggesting a carefree and spontaneous nature.
  • 冯海粟先生亦有鹧鸪天 - "Mr. Feng Haisu also has a 'Zhegu Tian' (Partridge Sky)," another piece of literature or art.
  • 鰕影薄防防见背纹轻细细浮 - "The shadow of a shrimp is thin and barely visible, the turtle's back pattern is light and fine," a detailed and poetic description of a natural scene.
  • 香雾敛翠云海霞为带月为钩 - "Fragrant mist gathers, green clouds form a belt, sea霞 forms a hook," a vivid and poetic description of a natural scene.
  • 夜来卷尽西山雨不着人间半防愁 - "At night, it rolls up all the rain from the West Mountain, leaving no trace of sorrow in the world," a metaphorical way of saying that the beauty of nature can cleanse one's heart of worries.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 司马温公 - Refers to Sima Guang, a prominent statesman and historian of the Song Dynasty, known for his contributions to Chinese historiography and governance.
  • 元祐之耆英 - Refers to the distinguished scholars and officials of the Yuanyou era (1086-1094) during the Song Dynasty, a period known for its cultural and intellectual achievements.
  • 永和之少长 - Refers to the young and old of the Yonghe era (1049-1054) during the Song Dynasty, another period of cultural and intellectual flourishing.
  • 珠帘秀 - A stage name or nickname for a performer, possibly a courtesan or actress known for her beauty and talent.
  • 杂剧 - Refers to a form of traditional Chinese theater that combines music, dance, and drama.
  • 胡紫山宣慰 - Refers to Mr. Hu Zishan, a high-ranking official responsible for maintaining order and peace.
  • 冯海粟先生 - Refers to Mr. Feng Haisu, a contemporary of the author, possibly a scholar or artist.
  • 鹧鸪天 - A type of Chinese poetry or song, often used in traditional Chinese literature.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 春衣典尽春寒峭 - All her spring clothes have been pawned, and the spring cold is sharp.
  • 忍将罗带拆同心 - How can she bear to untie the tied ribbons, symbolizing their union?
  • 懊恨浮生头白早 - Regretting the fleeting life and growing old too soon.
  • 珠钿翠靥幸仅存 - The pearl diadems and green cheek ornaments are still intact.
  • 此时犹及嫁夫君 - At this moment, she can still marry her husband.
  • 十二楼头燕子去挥手不可留 - The swallows fly away from the twelve-story tower, and no one can hold them back.
  • 仙裠去妾相悲两相约既去犹烦送肴酌 - When the fairy leaves, the woman is sad; they had agreed to part, but still, the food and wine must be offered.
  • 主君讵忍覆弃之见此翻令心绪恶 - How could the master bear to abandon her? Seeing this, it makes his mood even worse.
  • 一心专天天得知忍着主衣还事谁 - Her heart is devoted, and heaven knows; she cannot bear to wear the master's clothes for anyone else.
  • 遂携衾襥与俱来后君死者当为尼 - So she brings the quilt and bedding with her. If the master dies first, she will become a nun.
  • 不愿新懽恋旧恩千万人中两人耳 - She does not want new joy, but laments the old grace; she is one of the two people in a million.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 建迷井田 - Refers to the failed attempt to implement the well-field system (井田制) during the early Han dynasty, symbolizing failed reform.
  • 祖龙死 - Refers to the death of Qin Shi Huang, the 'First Emperor' (祖龙), a key figure in Chinese history.
  • 赤灵火徳明中天 - Likely refers to the Han dynasty's claim to the 'Red Fire' (赤帝) mandate, symbolizing their legitimacy.
  • 汉朝文景称至治刑措可比成康 - Refers to the reigns of Emperor Wen and Emperor Jing of Han, known for their prosperity and good governance, compared to the sage rulers Cheng and Kang of the Zhou dynasty.
  • 黄老术 - Refers to the Huang-Lao philosophy, a mix of Daoist and Legalist thought that influenced early Han governance.
  • 王莽贼臣簒汉祚 - Refers to Wang Mang, who usurped the Han dynasty and established the Xin dynasty.
  • 光武如周宣 - Refers to Emperor Guangwu of Han, who restored the Han dynasty, likened to the virtuous Zhou Xuanyuan (周宣王).
  • 云台名将应列宿 - Refers to the famous generals enshrined in the Cloud Platform (云台) at the Temple of the Martial God, symbolizing their stellar status.
  • 婉婉良防扶戎轩 - Likely refers to the support of loyal ministers in maintaining the Han dynasty.
  • 绝胜高祖彭越可比周召终天年 - Compares the achievements of Emperor Gaozu (Han dynasty founder) and his generals to the longevity of the Zhou dynasty's rulers.
  • 崇儒徃谒曲阜庙 - Refers to the veneration of Confucius at his temple in Qufu, a symbol of Confucianism's influence.
  • 典章灿灿罗星 - Describes the brilliance of Han dynasty institutions and rituals, likened to the stars.
  • 后人不省创业苦 - Criticizes later generations for forgetting the hardships of founding the dynasty.
  • 宠任阉宦皆貂蝉 - Refers to the corruption of the Han court by eunuchs, who were granted high ranks (貂蝉).
  • 西园粥爵诚可耻 - Refers to the scandalous sale of official positions in the Western Garden during the late Han dynasty.
  • 党锢忠士灾何延 - Refers to the persecution of loyal officials during the Party Prohibitions (党锢之祸).
  • 一朝曹氏帝称魏 - Refers to the rise of the Cao family and the establishment of the Wei dynasty.
  • 铜驼荆棘生荒烟 - A metaphor for the decline of the Han dynasty, with its symbols of power (copper camels) now overgrown with weeds.
  • 关张早死后主弱 - Refers to the early deaths of Guan Yu and Zhang Fei, weakening Liu Shan (the last emperor of Shu Han).
  • 典午自帝开坤埏 - Refers to the rise of the Cao family and the founding of the Wei dynasty.
  • 五方云扰乱中国 - Refers to the chaos caused by the Five Barbarians (五胡) during the Jin dynasty.
  • 五马南渡何翩翩 - Refers to the migration of the Eastern Jin court to the south, symbolized by the five horses of the imperial carriage.
  • 六朝兴废有得失 - Refers to the rise and fall of the Six Dynasties, each with its own successes and failures.
  • 岂知合并归杨坚 - Refers to the eventual reunification of China under the Sui dynasty, led by Yang Jian (Emperor Wen of Sui).
  • 琼花城里建 - Likely refers to the construction of a city or palace in the 'City of Precious Flowers,' a metaphor for the Sui dynasty's achievements.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 六朝 - The Six Dynasties (220–589 CE), a period of political fragmentation and cultural flourishing in southern China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 宫阙汴河春水浮龙船 - Poetic description of the imperial palace and imperial boats on the Bian River in spring, evoking the grandeur and prosperity of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 乱离思治否复泰 - Rhetorical question expressing the desire for order and peace after chaos and disorder.
  • 唐室高祖催飞骞 - Reference to Emperor Gaozu of Tang, who founded the Tang Dynasty.
  • 秦王神武不可及 - Reference to Li Shimin (Emperor Taizong), known for his military prowess and strategic brilliance.
  • 遂承天祚传高 - Indicates that the Tang Dynasty was established by divine mandate and passed down through generations.
  • 大纲不正有惭色 - Suggests that despite the dynasty's achievements, there were moral or political failings that caused shame.
  • 我尝抚卷思其渊 - Personal reflection on the depth and complexity of history.
  • 纷纷女祸握神器 - Refers to the influence of women in politics, particularly during the An Lushan Rebellion.
  • 扰扰藩镇横戈鋋 - Describes the chaos and conflict caused by regional military governors (fanzhen).
  • 乘舆避乱数奔窜 - Indicates the emperor's flight from the capital during times of turmoil.
  • 翠华几度游西川 - Refers to the imperial court's retreat to the west, particularly to Sichuan.
  • 黄巢残贼不忍説 - Reference to the rebellion led by Huang Chao, which caused immense destruction.
  • 白骨山积血成泉 - Vivid description of the devastation caused by war.

  • 我朝列圣皆深仁 - Refers to the Song Dynasty, emphasizing the benevolence of its emperors.
  • 天下苍生得苏息 - Indicates that the people of the world found relief and peace under the Song Dynasty.
  • 史书浩浩充屋栋 - Metaphor for the vast amount of historical records.
  • 人主欲观宁遍及 - Suggests that rulers should read history to learn from it.

  • 比美成王戒无逸 - Refers to the *Shijing* (Book of Songs) and the advice to avoid laziness and remain vigilant.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 紅门外留守司在焉 - Indicates that the '留守司' (Liu Shou Si, a governmental office) is located outside the '红门' (Red Gate). The structure '在焉' is a classical way of indicating location.
  • 西华南有仪鸾局 - '西华南' means 'to the southwest,' and '仪鸾局' refers to an official bureau responsible for ceremonial affairs. The phrase describes the location of this bureau.
  • 西有鹰房 - '鹰房' refers to a facility for keeping eagles, often used for falconry or hunting.
  • 厚载北为御苑 - '厚载' suggests a large or expansive area, and '御苑' means the imperial garden. The phrase indicates that the imperial garden is located to the north.
  • 外周垣红门十有五 - '外周垣' means the outer wall, and '红门十有五' indicates that there are fifteen red gates.
  • 内苑红门五 - '内苑' refers to the inner garden, and '红门五' means there are five red gates.
  • 御苑红门四 - '御苑' again refers to the imperial garden, and '红门四' means there are four red gates.
  • 此两垣之内也 - '此两垣之内也' means 'within these two walls.'
  • 大明门在崇天门内 - '崇天门' is a gate, and this phrase indicates that the '大明门' (Great Bright Gate) is located inside the '崇天门.'
  • 大明殿乃登极正旦寿节会朝之正衙也 - '登极' refers to the ascension to the throne, '正旦' is New Year's Day, '寿节' is the birthday celebration, and '会朝' means court gatherings. The phrase describes the '大明殿' as the main hall for these important occasions.
  • 十一间东西二百尺深一百二十尺高九十尺 - This describes the dimensions of the '大明殿': eleven bays wide, 200 feet long, 120 feet deep, and 90 feet high.
  • 柱廊七间深二百四十尺广四十四尺高五十尺 - This describes the dimensions of the columned corridor: seven bays wide, 240 feet long, 44 feet wide, and 50 feet high.
  • 寝室五间东西夹六间后连香阁三间 - This describes the layout of the sleeping quarters: five rooms, with six rooms on either side, followed by three rooms in a row.
  • 东西一百四十尺深五十尺高七十尺 - This describes the dimensions of the area: 140 feet wide, 50 feet deep, and 70 feet high.
  • 青石花础白玉石圎磶文石甃地 - '青石花础' refers to blue stone flower bases, '白玉石圎磶' refers to white marble round steps, and '文石甃地' means the ground is paved with patterned stones.
  • 上借重茵丹楹金饰龙绕其上 - '上借重茵' means the upper part is covered with heavy brocade, '丹楹' refers to red pillars, and '金饰龙绕其上' means golden dragons are carved around the pillars.
  • 四面朱锁窓藻井间金绘饰 - '四面朱锁窓' means the windows on all sides are painted red, and '藻井间金绘饰' refers to the ceiling decorated with gold paintings.
  • 燕石重陛朱防涂金铜飞雕冐中设七寳云龙御榻 - '燕石重陛' refers to the heavy steps made of fine stone, '朱防涂金' means red paint with gold accents, '铜飞雕冐' refers to bronze flying dragons, and '七寳云龙御榻' means the throne is adorned with seven treasures and cloud dragons.
  • 白盖金缕褥并设 - '白盖' refers to a white canopy, and '金缕褥' means a gold-threaded cushion.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 前乎此曷为未有志河源者 - A rhetorical question meaning 'Why, before this, has no one recorded the source of the river?'
  • 道路辽阻所传闻异辞 - Means 'the distant and difficult roads lead to varying accounts,' referring to the difficulty of obtaining accurate information due to geographical and communication barriers.
  • 莫能防河之源也 - Means 'no one could trace the source of the river,' indicating the historical challenge of locating the river's origin.
  • 山经曰敦薨之水西流注于泑泽 - A direct quotation from the *Shan Jing* (山经), describing the flow of the *Dunhong* (敦薨) water westward into the *Yaoze* (泑泽).
  • 实惟河源而水经载河出昆仑 - The *Shan Jing* identifies this as the source of the Yellow River, while the *Shui Jing* (水经) claims the Yellow River originates from *Kunlun* (昆仑), creating a contradiction.
  • 穆天子传亦云阳纡之山河曰冯夷所居 - Another reference to the *Mu Tianzi Zhuan* (穆天子传), which states that the *Yangyu* (阳纡) mountain is where the river god *Fengyi* (冯夷) resides.
  • 释氏西域志称阿耨达大山上有大渊水即昆仑山也 - A reference to Buddhist texts, which identify *Anuoda* (阿耨达) as the mountain with a large body of water, equated with *Kunlun* (昆仑).
  • 地里志亦称昆仑山在临羌西 - Another reference to *Dili Zhi* (地里志), which places *Kunlun* (昆仑) to the west of *Lin Qiang* (临羌).
  • 汉书载河出两源或称有或称无 - A reference to the *Han Shu* (汉书), which records conflicting accounts of the Yellow River having two sources.

  • 夏取甘肃等城秋取西凉府遂过沙陀至黄河九渡 - A description of the military campaign, capturing cities like *Gansu* (甘肃) and *Xiliang* (西凉), crossing *Shatuo* (沙陀), and reaching the ninth crossing of the Yellow River.
  • 昆仑当九渡下流则昆仑固已归我职方氏矣 - A statement that *Kunlun* (昆仑) is now under the jurisdiction of the Yuan dynasty, as it lies downstream of the ninth crossing of the Yellow River.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sentence '君相别而出时坐中一千户邀李入茶坊告之曰' must be read as a sequence of actions: the two parties part, the thousand households invite Li, and then they tell him something.
  • The long sentence '十五年前吾亦无子因到都置一婢则已有孕到家时适吾妻亦有孕前后一两月间各生一男' describes a series of events over time, including the purchase of a pregnant maid and the subsequent pregnancy of Li's wife.
  • The clause '今皆十五六矣岂君之子也' introduces a question about the identity of the sons.
  • The clause '两人各言妇人之容貌岁齿相同' describes the similarity between the two women.
  • The clause '李归语于妻妻往日诚悍妒心颇惭而怜之' describes the wife's change in attitude.
  • The clause '翼日邀千户至家享以盛馔与之刻期而别' describes a formal hospitality gesture and a planned separation.
  • The clause '千户先归南阳府李以实告于所管近侍大官乞假前往' describes Li's formal request and the thousand households' actions.
  • The clause '此美事也我当与汝奏闻' shows a positive evaluation and a commitment to help.
  • The clause '既而有防得给驿以行凡筵席之费皆从官办' describes the formal approval and financial support.
  • The clause '李至众官郊迎设大宴李所以馈献千户并其妻子防妾之物甚侈' describes a formal welcome and a grand banquet.

  • 先辈风致 - Means 'the style of the elders,' introducing a story about a respected scholar.

  • 酒半宪使举杯请曰今日之欢皆玉带为也愿先生酬之以诗 - Means 'Halfway through the meal, the constitutional official raised his cup and said, 'Today's joy is all due to Xiao Yu. I wish for you to compose a poem in her honor,' showing the official's request.
  • 先生其毋辞 - Means 'Please do not refuse,' urging the scholar to accept the request.
  • 时先生负海内重名雅畏清议 - Means 'At that time, the scholar was famous throughout the country and feared public opinion,' indicating his reputation and caution.
  • 又不能违宪使之请遂书一絶句云 - Means 'And he could not refuse the official's request, so he wrote a quatrain,' describing the scholar's decision to comply.
  • 菡蓞池边风满衣木樨亭下雨霏霏 - The first line of the quatrain, meaning 'By the lotus pond, the wind fills my clothes; under the osmanthus pavilion, rain falls lightly,' setting a poetic scene.
  • 老夫记得坡仙语病体难禁玉带围 - The second line of the quatrain, meaning 'Old man, I remember the words of the Immortal Su (Su Shi), 'My sick body cannot withstand the jade belt's embrace,' quoting a famous line from Su Shi's poetry.
  • 于是举席称叹尽欢而散 - Means 'Thus, everyone at the table praised and admired, and they parted in high spirits,' describing the positive reception of the poem.
  • 盖前辈既不肯拂人意又不欲失所守而且用事清切一时风致可想见信非野儒俗士所能及也 - Means 'Indeed, the elder scholars neither wished to offend others' intentions nor wanted to lose their principles, and they used clear and precise allusions.   • 后余亦一小者状若旋环制作大约相似 - Later, I also made a smaller one, shaped like a ring, with a similar construction.

  • 其玉具劒自三代有之今止以两汉为始至于宋朝且千余年未有能穷其辘轳底蕴今偶以煮脱乃得其机轴亦云竒矣 - The jade-handled sword has existed since the Three Dynasties, but it is only from the Han dynasty that it is recorded; up to the Song dynasty, no one has been able to fully understand its internal mechanism, but now, by cooking and removing it, I have discovered its mechanism, which is indeed remarkable.

  • 其文曰制曰严夘帝命莫忘日资唯是黒青白黄既正既直既觚既方庶使罔谈莫我敢当 - The text says: 'Order: Yan Ma, the emperor's command must not be forgotten. Only black, blue, white, and yellow are correct, straight, square, and proper. Thus, no one dares to speak falsely.'
  • 与前严夘铭词并差鲜于伯机经歴一枚高彦敬尚书二枚并真楷书皆似近代制作未见所出 - Compared to the previous Yan Ma inscriptions, there are differences. One is from the experiences of Xian Yu Boji, and two are from the deeds of Gao Yanjing, both written in true regular script, resembling modern creations, but their origins are unknown.
  • 按五纽绳器也兎罟也岂纫丝绳与兎罟相故同此音耶 - According to this, it is a five-knot rope device and a rabbit net. Is it that the twisted rope and rabbit net are similar, hence the same pronunciation?
  • 又马永乡真子録云汉人以正月卯日作佩之铭其一面曰刚夘乃知今人立春或戴春胜亦古制也盖刚者强也夘者刘也正月佩之尊国姓也与陈汤所谓强汉者同义 - Also, according to the 'True Son's Record' of Ma Yongxiang, the Han people made a pendant to wear on the day of the second month of the first lunar month. One side says 'Gang Ma'. Thus, we know that people today wear spring decorations on the first day of spring, which is also an ancient practice. 'Gang' means strong, and 'Ma' means Liu (the surname of the Han dynasty). Wearing it in the first month shows respect for the national surname, which is the same as what Chen Tang called the 'Strong Han'.

'虞邵庵先生' is a person's name, '初以为诳' means he initially thought it was a lie, '及质诸其土人之在京师者则始信' means he verified it with locals in the capital and then believed it, '盖其人习以为常不以为异耳' means that the locals were accustomed to it and found it normal.
  • 杭州属邑有一巨室怙财挟势虐害良善邑官贪墨莫敢谁何众不可堪走诉宪府巨室逃匿宪使怒督责有司示罪赏揭大逵且家至壁白蔵者罪连坐首捕者赏万缗其友人王一山者世业儒居湖山第一楼帱彼于宻期月不发隣家察知图给赏钱告报于官官搜索得之倂王逮系囚见宪使使问云女知彼所犯乎王曰知之女闻国有制乎曰知之女见揭示罪赏乎曰见之女奚不就利避害乎曰朋友颠连来奔乘其危以售之则名教中有所不容某诚弗忍为事觉连坐乃甘心焉使竦然曰君子所谓临难毋茍免其人践之矣真义士也若加以罪是吾政苛而刑滥民何以劝遂释之使即许文正公子也 - Describes a series of events involving a wealthy family, a corrupt official, and a righteous individual. '杭州属邑' refers to a county under Hangzhou, '巨室' means a wealthy family, '怙财挟势' means to rely on wealth and power, '虐害良善' means to harm the good, '邑官贪墨' means corrupt local officials, '莫敢谁何' means no one dared to do anything, '走诉宪府' means to appeal to the provincial governor, '巨室逃匿' means the wealthy family fled, '宪使怒督责有司' means the governor was angry and ordered the officials to act, '示罪赏揭大逵' means to post rewards for catching the criminals, '家至壁白蔵者罪连坐' means that those who hid in their homes would be punished along with the criminals, '首捕者赏万缗' means the first to capture the criminals would receive a reward of ten thousand strings of cash, '其友人王一山者世业儒居湖山第一楼' means Wang Yishan, a scholar by profession, lived in the first floor of a lake and mountain complex, '帱彼于宻期月不发' means he covered for him for a long time without reporting, '隣家察知图给赏钱告报于官' means a neighbor discovered this and wanted the reward, '官搜索得之倂王逮系囚' means the officials searched and caught both of them, '见宪使使问云女知彼所犯乎' means they were questioned by the governor, '知之女闻国有制乎' means they knew about the country's laws, '见揭示罪赏乎' means they saw the posted rewards, '奚不就利避害乎' means why not take advantage of the situation to avoid harm, '朋友颠连来奔乘其危以售之则名教中有所不容' means that helping a friend in danger would be against moral teachings, '某诚弗忍为事觉连坐乃甘心焉' means he was unwilling to do it and was willing to face the consequences, '使竦然曰君子所谓临难毋茍免其人践之矣' means the governor was impressed and said that the person had lived up to the saying of not shirking danger, '真义士也若加以罪是吾政苛而刑滥民何以劝遂释之' means he was a true righteous man, and punishing him would be excessive and discourage the people, so he was released, '使即许文正公子也' means the governor was actually Xu Wenzheng's son.

  • 武侯相汉 - Refers to Zhuge Liang's role as the chancellor of Shu Han during the Three Kingdoms period.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秦皇坑儒 - Historical event in Chinese history where Emperor Qin Shi Huang ordered the execution of scholars and the burning of books.
  • 武侯相汉 - Refers to Zhuge Liang's role as the chancellor of Shu Han during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 李斯 - Prominent statesman of the Qin Dynasty, known for his role in the unification of China and the implementation of Legalist policies.
  • 始皇三十二年 - Refers to the 32nd year of Emperor Qin Shi Huang's reign, a period marked by significant historical events.
  • 卢生 - A figure associated with the Qin Dynasty, known for his prophecies and advice to Emperor Qin Shi Huang.

[543]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 真人至愿 - Refers to the ultimate wish or desire of a true person or sage.
  • 毋令人知 - Means 'do not let anyone know.'
  • 不死之药 - Refers to the elixir of immortality.
  • 建阿房宫 - Refers to the construction of the A-Fang Palace.
  • 千间万落 - Describes a vast and extensive palace complex.
  • 稔其恶 - Means 'to harbor or nurture evil.'
  • 纵臾之 - Means 'to encourage or incite.'
  • 方伎之流 - Refers to practitioners of various arts and techniques, often including alchemists and immortals.
  • 儒者 - Refers to Confucian scholars or practitioners.
  • 封禅之议 - Refers to the ritual of Fengshan, a ceremony performed by emperors to worship heaven and earth.
  • 谤口纷纷 - Means 'numerous slanderous words.'
  • 懐杀意 - Means 'to harbor murderous intent.'
  • 坑之 - Means 'to bury alive' or 'to execute.'
  • 天理之不容 - Means 'not tolerated by heavenly justice.'
  • 海上 - Refers to the sea or the eastern seas where the elixir of immortality was sought.
  • 童男童女 - Refers to young boys and girls.
  • 无告之孤 - Refers to orphans with no one to protect them.
  • 肆厥不轨之状 - Means 'to act in an unrestrained and lawless manner.'
  • 妖教 - Refers to a false or heretical teaching.
  • 死无辜者多矣 - Means 'many innocent people have died.'
  • 兴北伐之师 - Means 'to launch a northern campaign.'
  • 筑长城 - Refers to the construction of the Great Wall.
  • 断地脉 - Means 'to sever the earth's veins' or 'to disrupt the natural flow of the land.'
  • 南北生灵 - Refers to the people living in the north and south.
  • 因是役而死者不可胜算 - Means 'those who died as a result of this labor cannot be counted.'
  • 骸积如山 - Means 'bones piled up like mountains.'
  • 血流成川 - Means 'blood flowed like rivers.'
  • 调发频仍 - Means 'frequent mobilization or conscription.'
  • 剥及闾左 - Means 'to strip the people of the left side of the street' (a euphemism for heavy taxation or forced labor).
  • 原始要终 - Means 'to trace the origin and conclude the end.'
  • 谁生厉阶 - Means 'who created the cause of trouble.'
  • 献辟之术 - Refers to the practice of exorcism or the expulsion of ghosts.
  • 觊真人之来 - Means 'to hope for the arrival of a true person.'
  • 咸阳宫 - Refers to the palace in Xianyang.
  • 二百七十道 - Refers to 270 paths or roads leading to the palace, symbolizing the extensive network of infrastructure.
  • 其语一 - Means 'the words are one' or 'the statement is consistent.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 真人 - Refers to a sage or enlightened person, often associated with Daoist immortals.
  • 阿房宫 - A grand imperial palace built by Emperor Qin Shi Huang.
  • 卢生 - A famous alchemist and magician during the Qin Dynasty, known for his involvement in the search for the elixir of immortality.
  • 封禅 - A significant ritual performed by emperors to demonstrate their virtue and connection to heaven and earth.
  • 坑之 - Refers to the Massacre of the Scholars, where Confucian scholars were buried alive by Qin Shi Huang.
  • 妖教 - A term used to describe false or heretical teachings, often associated with religious or magical practices that were deemed harmful.
  • 咸阳宫 - The main palace in Xianyang, the capital of the Qin Dynasty.
  • 二百七十道 - Refers to the 270 paths or roads leading to the palace, symbolizing the extensive network of infrastructure.

Proper Nouns

  • 真人 - True person or sage.
  • 阿房宫 - A-Fang Palace.
  • 卢生 - Lu Sheng.
  • 封禅 - Fengshan ritual.
  • 坑之 - Massacre of the Scholars.
  • 妖教 - False or heretical teaching.
  • 咸阳宫 - Palace in Xianyang.
  • 二百七十道 - 270 paths or roads.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 泄时在旁者尽杀之 - Those present at the time of the event were killed.
  • 自是莫知行之所在 - From then on, no one knew where he was.
  • 此三罪也 - These are three crimes.
  • 有一于此罪不容于死 - If there is one of these, the crime cannot be forgiven by death.
  • 况兼有之 - Especially when combined.
  • 以四百六十余人之坑偿万人之命 - To compensate for the lives of ten thousand people with the burial of four hundred and sixty plus individuals.
  • 良不为过 - It is not excessive.
  • 天网恢恢防而不漏 - The net of heaven is vast and prevents nothing from escaping.
  • 真可畏哉 - Truly to be feared.
  • 卢生等吾尊赐之甚厚 - Lu Sheng and others were greatly honored by me.
  • 今乃诽谤我 - Now they slander me.
  • 诸生在咸阳者吾使廉问 - I have instructed the officials in Xianyang to investigate the scholars.
  • 或为妖言以乱黔首 - Some speak of strange words to confuse the common people.
  • 于是使御史按问诸生 - Thus I have ordered the Censor to question the scholars.
  • 传相告引 - They pass on information and incriminate each other.
  • 仆亦信卢生非吾儒中人 - I also believe that Lu Sheng is not among our Confucian scholars.
  • 自扶苏言之误使儒者不韪之名 - From the perspective of Fu Su, his words mistakenly led to the scholars being blamed.
  • 自我一洗亦万世之快也 - To cleanse this from myself is also a great joy for all time.
  • 不然如两 - This phrase is incomplete and unclear, possibly indicating a comparison or contrast.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 始皇 - Refers to Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China.
  • 卢生 - A historical figure, a Daoist alchemist and advisor to Qin Shi Huang.
  • 扶苏 - The eldest son of Qin Shi Huang, who was favored by Confucian scholars.
  • 儒者 - Refers to Confucian scholars or followers of Confucius.
  • 坑儒 - Refers to the historical event where Confucian scholars were buried alive, often associated with Qin Shi Huang's reign.
  • 黔首 - Refers to the common people, a term used in ancient China.
  • 御史 - Refers to the Censor, an official responsible for investigating and reporting on misconduct.
  • 廉问 - Refers to the act of investigating or inquiring into matters.

Proper Nouns

  • 始皇 - Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China.
  • 卢生 - A historical figure, a Daoist alchemist and advisor to Qin Shi Huang.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 生四皓伏生之流 - Refers to the Four Sages and Fu Sheng, two figures known for their scholarly contributions. '流' means 'of the same kind or class.'
  • 鸿飞冥冥 - An idiom meaning 'flying high into the vast sky,' often used to describe someone who is far away or unreachable.
  • 弋人何慕 - A phrase meaning 'what does a hunter desire?' used to express disdain for those who pursue trivial or base goals.
  • 肯揺唇鼓吻 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to speak or argue at length,' often with a negative connotation.
  • 自投于防穽 - A phrase meaning 'to throw oneself into a trap,' used to describe someone who voluntarily enters a dangerous or harmful situation.
  • 仆故曰 - A phrase meaning 'I therefore say,' used to introduce a conclusion or summary.
  • 卢生四百六十余人皆方伎之士也 - A statement indicating that over 400 individuals associated with Lu Sheng were all skilled in various arts and techniques.
  • 天下之大 - A phrase meaning 'the vastness of the world,' often used to emphasize the scale of something.
  • 所谓儒者固不止此 - A phrase meaning 'so-called scholars are certainly not limited to this,' suggesting that there are more scholars beyond those mentioned.
  • 其坑之者此而已矣 - A phrase meaning 'those who were buried alive are only these,' indicating that the victims were limited to a specific group.
  • 有道之士秦不能坑 - A statement meaning 'scholars of virtue could not be buried alive,' implying that virtuous individuals were spared.
  • 火德一炎 - A phrase referring to the 'fire element' in the Five Elements theory, often associated with the Qin dynasty.
  • 两生以讲礼闻 - A phrase meaning 'the two scholars were known for their expertise in ritual,' referring to the two scholars who specialized in rituals.
  • 四皓以羽翼之功闻 - A phrase meaning 'the Four Sages were known for their support of the royal family,' referring to the Four Sages who supported the imperial family.
  • 伏生以口授古书闻 - A phrase meaning 'Fu Sheng was known for orally transmitting ancient texts,' referring to Fu Sheng's role in preserving and teaching ancient texts.
  • 留此数公以见吾儒不可磨灭 - A phrase meaning 'to leave these few scholars to demonstrate that our Confucianism is indestructible,' emphasizing the enduring nature of Confucian thought.
  • 商君以变法祸秦 - A phrase meaning 'Shang Yang brought disaster to the Qin through his legal reforms,' referring to Shang Yang's reforms that led to his downfall.
  • 竟遭车裂 - A phrase meaning 'he was eventually subjected to the punishment of being torn apart,' referring to the execution method used on Shang Yang.
  • 卢生以方伎祸秦 - A phrase meaning 'Lu Sheng brought disaster to the Qin through his skills,' referring to Lu Sheng's influence that led to his own demise.
  • 坑于咸阳 - A phrase meaning 'he was buried alive in Xianyang,' referring to the location where Lu Sheng was executed.
  • 其罪等也 - A phrase meaning 'their sins are equal,' suggesting that both Shang Yang and Lu Sheng faced similar fates due to their actions.
  • 天其或者假手于秦欤 - A phrase meaning 'perhaps heaven used the Qin as a tool,' suggesting that the Qin's actions were part of a larger divine plan.
  • 商君裂矣卢生坑矣 - A phrase meaning 'Shang Yang was torn apart, and Lu Sheng was buried alive,' summarizing their fates.
  • 而秦以不祀 - A phrase meaning 'and the Qin dynasty ceased to exist,' indicating the end of the Qin dynasty.
  • 抑亦自相挤防之明报而祸淫之道为不偏矣 - A phrase meaning 'or perhaps it was a clear retribution for their mutual destruction and the path of disaster for those who indulge in vice,' suggesting that the Qin's downfall was a result of their own actions.
  • 仆恶夫坑儒之名 - A phrase meaning 'I dislike the name 'burying scholars alive,' expressing the author's disapproval of the term.
  • 故论其颠末如此 - A phrase meaning 'therefore, I discuss the details as such,' introducing the author's explanation.
  • 文豹曰 - A phrase meaning 'Wen Bao says,' introducing a quote from Wen Bao.
  • 古今论孔明者莫不以忠义许之 - A phrase meaning 'throughout history, those who discuss Zhuge Liang have always praised him for his loyalty and righteousness,' praising Zhuge Liang's character.
  • 然余兄文龙尝考其颠末 - A phrase meaning 'however, my brother Wen Long once examined the details,' indicating that Wen Long conducted a detailed study.
  • 以为孔明之才 - A phrase meaning 'he believed that Zhuge Liang's talent,' suggesting that Wen Long had a particular view on Zhuge Liang's abilities.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 四皓 - Refers to the Four Sages, four elderly scholars who supported the Han dynasty.
  • 伏生 - Refers to Fu Sheng, a scholar who preserved and taught ancient texts during the Qin dynasty.
  • 卢生 - Refers to Lu Sheng, a scholar and alchemist who was executed by the Qin dynasty.
  • 商君 - Refers to Shang Yang, a reformer of the Qin dynasty who implemented significant legal and administrative changes.
  • 咸阳 - Refers to Xianyang, the capital city of the Qin dynasty.
  • 孔明 - Refers to Zhuge Liang, a prominent statesman and military strategist of the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 文龙 - Refers to Wen Long, the brother of Wen Bao, who conducted a detailed study of Zhuge Liang.

Proper Nouns

  • 四皓 - The Four Sages.
  • 伏生 - Fu Sheng.
  • 卢生 - Lu Sheng.
  • 商君 - Shang Yang.
  • 咸阳 - Xianyang.
  • 孔明 - Zhuge Liang.
  • 文龙 - Wen Long.

[546]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 谓之识时务则可谓之明大义则未也 - Contrasts two evaluations: being called 'wise in worldly affairs' versus being called 'clear in great principles.'
  • 谓之忠于刘备则可谓之忠于汉室则未也 - Contrasts 'loyalty to Liu Bei' with 'loyalty to the Han dynasty,' implying they are not equivalent.
  • 其说有四 - Means 'there are four reasons' or 'four points of argument.'
  • 一者备虽称为中山靖王之后然其服属疏逺世数难攷 - Introduces the first point with '一者,' and '虽称为' means 'although called.'
  • 温公谓犹宋高祖自称楚元王后故通鉴不敢以绍汉统 - Sima Guang's reasoning: like Emperor Song Gaozu claiming to be a descendant of King Yuan of Chu, the *Zizhi Tongjian* does not regard him as the successor to the Han dynasty.
  • 况备又非人望之所归 - Uses '况' to introduce a further point, meaning 'let alone.'
  • 周瑜以枭雄目之刘巴以谁人视之司马懿以诈力鄙之孙权以猾虏呼之 - Lists different opinions using parallel structures.
  • 亮独何见而委身焉 - Uses a rhetorical question to introduce a further point.
  • 借使以为刘氏族属然献帝在上犹当如光武之事更始东征西伐一切听命焉可也 - Uses a conditional structure to introduce a hypothetical scenario.
  • 二者备之枉驾草庐也 - Introduces the second point with '二者.'
  • 始谋不过曰主上尘孤不度德量力欲伸大义于天下 - Uses a concessive structure to introduce a contrast.
  • 其辞甚正其志甚伟 - Uses parallel structures to describe Liu Bei's words and aspirations.
  • 自亮开之以跨荆益成覇业之利而备之志向始移无复以献帝为念 - Uses a causal structure to introduce a change in Liu Bei's ambitions.
  • 由建安举兵以来二十四年天子或都许 - Uses a temporal structure to introduce a historical context.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 中山靖王 - Liu Sheng, the King of Zhongshan, an ancestor of Liu Bei.
  • 献帝 - Emperor Xian of Han, the last emperor of the Han dynasty.
  • 宋高祖 - Liu Yu, the founder of the Southern Song dynasty.
  • 楚元王 - Liu Sheng, the King of Chu, another ancestor of Liu Yu.
  • 周瑜 - A famous general and strategist during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 刘巴 - A strategist and official during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 司马懿 - A prominent statesman and military leader during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 孙权 - The founder of the Eastern Wu state during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 光武 - Emperor Guangwu of Han, who founded the Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 更始 - Emperor Gengshi of Han, who briefly ruled after the fall of the Western Han dynasty.
  • 建安举兵 - Refers to the start of the Three Kingdoms period, marked by the rebellion led by Cao Cao in 196 AD.

Proper Nouns

  • 中山靖王 - Liu Sheng, the King of Zhongshan.
  • 献帝 - Emperor Xian of Han.
  • 宋高祖 - Liu Yu, the founder of the Southern Song dynasty.
  • 楚元王 - Liu Sheng, the King of Chu.
  • 周瑜 - A famous general and strategist during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 刘巴 - A strategist and official during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 司马懿 - A prominent statesman and military leader during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 孙权 - The founder of the Eastern Wu state during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 光武 - Emperor Guangwu of Han.
  • 更始 - Emperor Gengshi of Han.
  • 建安举兵 - Refers to the start of the Three Kingdoms period.

[547]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 或居长安或幸洛阳 - Sometimes residing in Chang'an or being favored in Luoyang, indicating the shifting locations of the court.
  • 宫室煨烬越在篱间 - Palaces reduced to ashes, now only a few walls standing, describing the destruction and decay of the imperial palaces.
  • 备未尝使一介行李诣行在所 - The lord never sent even a single messenger to the emperor's temporary residence, highlighting the lack of communication.
  • 今年合众万余明年合众三万 - This year, he gathered over ten thousand followers; next year, he gathered thirty thousand, showing the rapid growth of his following.
  • 未尝一言禀命朝廷 - He never once sought permission from the court, emphasizing his independence.
  • 而亮亦未尝一谈及焉 - And Zhuge Liang never once mentioned it, indicating a shared silence on the matter.
  • 盖其帝蜀之心已定于草庐一见之时矣 - His heart to establish a kingdom in Shu was already set at the first meeting in the thatched cottage, referring to the famous 'Three Visits to the Thatched Cottage'.
  • 三者曹操欲顺流东下求救于吴无一言及献帝而独说以鼎足之说 - The third point is that Cao Cao, wanting to advance eastward down the river and seek help from Wu, did not mention Emperor Xian but instead spoke of the strategy of a tripod, referring to the strategic alliance between Sun Quan and Liu Bei.
  • 夫鼎足之说始于蒯通 - The strategy of a tripod began with Kuai Tong, mentioning the historical precedent.
  • 然通之说韩信以此犹有汉之一足当三国时而为是说则献帝无复染指之望矣 - However, when Kuai Tong advised Han Xin, it still left one leg of the tripod for Han. But during the Three Kingdoms period, if such a strategy were used, Emperor Xian would have no chance of reclaiming power, contrasting the different contexts.
  • 頼周瑜汉贼之骂足以激怒孙权故能成赤壁之胜 - Thanks to Zhou Yu's scolding of Cao Cao as a traitor, which sufficiently enraged Sun Quan, thus enabling the victory at the Battle of Red Cliffs, highlighting the role of Zhou Yu.
  • 若亮若备何以厉将士之气服曹操之心哉 - If Zhuge Liang and Liu Bei had done so, how could they have inspired the soldiers' morale and convinced Cao Cao? questioning the effectiveness of their approach.
  • 荆楚之士从之如云非从备也乃从汉也 - The people of Jing and Chu followed him like clouds, not following Liu Bei but following Han, emphasizing the loyalty to the Han dynasty.
  • 四者备之称王汉中则建安二十四年也献帝在上而敢于自王及称帝武担则闻献帝之遇害也 - The fourth point is that Liu Bei declared himself king of Hanzhong in the twenty-fourth year of Jian'an, despite Emperor Xian being still on the throne, and later declared himself emperor upon hearing of Emperor Xian's death, detailing the timing and context of Liu Bei's declarations.
  • 亮不能如董公说高 - Zhuge Liang could not be like Dong Gong in persuading Gao, referring to the historical figure Dong Gong and his role in persuading Gao.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 长安 - Chang'an, the capital of the Western Han and early Western Jin dynasties.
  • 洛阳 - Luoyang, the capital of the Eastern Han and several other dynasties.
  • 宫室 - Imperial palaces, symbolizing the power and glory of the Han dynasty.
  • 煨烬 - Ashes, indicating the destruction of the palaces.
  • 篱 - Walls, symbolizing the remnants of the imperial palaces.
  • 备 - Liu Bei, a key figure in the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 亮 - Zhuge Liang, Liu Bei's chief advisor.
  • 曹操 - Cao Cao, a powerful warlord and the founder of the Wei state.
  • 献帝 - Emperor Xian, the last emperor of the Han dynasty.
  • 吴 - Wu, one of the three states in the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 鼎足之说 - The strategy of a tripod, referring to the balance of power among the three states.
  • 蒯通 - Kuai Tong, a strategist during the Chu-Han Contention.
  • 韩信 - Han Xin, a famous general during the Chu-Han Contention.
  • 周瑜 - Zhou Yu, a general of the Wu state.
  • 赤壁之胜 - The Battle of Red Cliffs, a decisive battle in the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 荆楚 - Jing and Chu, regions in central China.
  • 汉中 - Hanzhong, a region in modern-day Shaanxi province.
  • 建安二十四年 - The twenty-fourth year of the Jian'an era (219 AD).
  • 武担 - Wudan, a place where Liu Bei declared himself emperor.
  • 董公 - Dong Gong, a historical figure known for his persuasive abilities.
  • 高 - Gao, a historical figure who was persuaded by Dong Gong.

Proper Nouns

  • 长安 - Chang'an.
  • 洛阳 - Luoyang.
  • 宫室 - Imperial palaces.
  • 备 - Liu Bei.
  • 亮 - Zhuge Liang.
  • 曹操 - Cao Cao.
  • 献帝 - Emperor Xian.

[548]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 祖率三軍為義帝缟素 - Formal statement indicating the leader (祖) led the three armies in mourning attire for the Righteous Emperor (義帝).
  • 仗大义连孙吴声罪讨贼 - Uphold great righteousness by allying with Sun and Wu to declare crimes and punish the traitors.
  • 遽乘此即帝位而反锋攻吴 - Hasty move to seize the imperial throne and then turn the army against Wu.
  • 晋文公有言父死之谓何又因以为利 - Saying by Duke Wen of Jin questioning what it means when a father dies and how it can be used for personal gain.
  • 故费诗以为大敌未克便先自立恐人心疑惑而谏以高祖不敢王秦之事 - Fei Shi believed that before defeating the major enemy, one should not hastily proclaim oneself king, fearing that it would confuse the people, and he advised against the precedent set by Liu Bang.
  • 亮反怒而黜之 - Zhuge Liang became angry and demoted Fei Shi.
  • 夫以操之奸雄其王其公犹必待天子之命 - Even a cunning and powerful figure like Cao Cao would still need the emperor's command to become a king or a duke.
  • 荀彧且以此愤死 - Xun You died of grief over this matter.
  • 备虽称宗室而亦臣也何所禀命而自王自帝 - Liu Bei, though claiming to be of the imperial lineage, was still a subject, and thus had no authority to proclaim himself king or emperor.
  • 固方哓哓以兴复汉室为辞 - Liu Bei insisted on the pretext of restoring the Han dynasty.
  • 不知兴复汉室为献帝耶为刘备耶 - Questions whether restoring the Han dynasty refers to Emperor Xian or Liu Bei.
  • 万一果能兴复将置献帝于何地 - If Liu Bei truly restores the Han dynasty, what will happen to Emperor Xian?
  • 出师一表虽忠诚恳恳特忠于所事耳其于大义实有所未明也 - The 'Expedition Manifesto' is filled with sincere and earnest words, but it only shows loyalty to one's cause and lacks clarity on great righteousness.
  • 管仲乐毅之事君子所羞道者以其但知有燕齐而不知有王室也 - Actions of Guan Zhong and Yue Yi, which are considered shameful by gentlemen because they only knew about Yan and Qi but not about the royal house.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以管乐自许 - Classical expression meaning 'to consider oneself as capable as Guan Zhong and Yue Yi,' referring to historical figures known for their political and military achievements.
  • 志虑之所图 - Means 'the thoughts and plans one devises,' referring to one's ambitions and strategies.
  • 功业之所成就 - Means 'the accomplishments and achievements one attains,' referring to one's successes and legacies.
  • 止于区区一蜀耳 - Means 'limited to a small place like Shu,' indicating a modest or limited scope.
  • 或者但为备刘氏宗也 - Means 'perhaps it was just to serve as a guardian of the Liu family,' suggesting a protective role rather than a claim to power.
  • 备帝蜀则汉祚存矣 - Means 'If Liu Bei were to rule Shu, the Han dynasty would continue,' implying that Liu Bei's rule would preserve the Han legacy.
  • 亮忠于备即忠于汉矣 - Means 'Zhuge Liang's loyalty to Liu Bei is loyalty to the Han dynasty,' suggesting that Zhuge Liang's allegiance is aligned with the Han cause.
  • 无献帝则可 - Means 'If there were no Emperor Xian, then it would be acceptable,' implying that the lack of a legitimate emperor would justify certain actions.
  • 有献帝在而君臣自相推戴 - Means 'If Emperor Xian were still alive and the rulers and ministers crowned each other,' suggesting a self-proclaimed legitimacy.
  • 赤眉之立盆子亦有辞于世矣 - Means 'The Red Eyebrows' establishment of Liu Pengze (Liu Yan) also had a justification in the world,' referring to the Red Eyebrows' claim to power.
  • 春秋之末诸侯争强周室微弱 - Means 'At the end of the Spring and Autumn period, the feudal lords competed for power while the Zhou royal house grew weak,' referring to the decline of the Zhou dynasty.
  • 孔子无一日不以尊王为心 - Means 'Confucius never ceased to have the heart of honoring the king,' indicating Confucius's loyalty to the Zhou king.
  • 若如亮之见 - Means 'If it were as Zhuge Liang saw it,' referring to Zhuge Liang's perspective.
  • 鲁同姓也亦可奉之为王矣 - Means 'Even if the state of Lu were a branch of the royal family, it could also be made a king,' suggesting that a lesser state could claim kingship.
  • 天下后世惟持此见 - Means 'The world and future generations only hold this view,' indicating a widespread acceptance of a particular perspective.
  • 于亮之事无敢置异议于其间 - Means 'There was no one who dared to raise objections to Zhuge Liang's actions,' suggesting universal approval.
  • 文中子曰通也敢忘大皇昭烈之懿识孔明公瑾之盛心 - Means 'The Middle Sage (Wang Tong) said, 'How can I forget the noble virtues of the great emperor Liu Bei and the great minds of Zhuge Liang and Zhou Yu?'' indicating respect and admiration.
  • 汉之君既称献帝魏之君又称武帝吴之君又称大皇帝蜀之君又称昭烈皇帝 - Means 'The Han emperor was called Emperor Xian, the Wei emperor was called Emperor Wu, the Wu emperor was called Great Emperor, and the Shu emperor was called Emperor Liu Bei,' referring to the titles of the emperors of the Three Kingdoms.
  • 天无二日民无二王 - Means 'There cannot be two suns in the sky or two kings for the people,' indicating the principle of a single ruler.
  • 一天下而四帝并立可乎 - Means 'Can the world be unified while four emperors coexist?' questioning the legitimacy of multiple rulers.
  • 通之见如此宜其为续书之僣也 - Means 'Given Wang Tong's view, it is appropriate that he is considered a heretic in the continuation of the classics,' suggesting that his perspective is controversial.
  • 余兄尝以是说取解于同文馆 - Means 'My brother once used this argument to explain it at the Tongwen Guan,' indicating a historical context of academic discussion.

  • 玺玉者印也 - Means 'a seal made of jade is an official stamp,' defining the nature of a seal.
  • 以守土故为文从尔从土 - Means 'because it is used to guard territory, the character is composed of 'you' and 'earth,' explaining the etymology of the character.
  • 其义盖曰天付尔此器俾寳之以守尔土也 - Means 'the meaning is that heaven has given you this object to treasure and guard your territory,' explaining the symbolic significance of the seal.
  • 至周太史籀易为从尔从玉 - Means 'up to the time of the Zhou historian Zhou who changed it to 'you' and 'jade,' indicating a historical change in the character.
  • 义取天付尔此玉寳以为天下君也 - Means 'the meaning is that heaven has given you this jade treasure to be the ruler of the world,' explaining the new significance of the seal.
  • 三代以上玺文无所考诸 - Means 'there is no record of seal inscriptions before the Three Dynasties,' indicating a lack of historical evidence.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 器而神寳 - Refers to a sacred or divine treasure, often associated with spiritual or supernatural significance.
  • 瑞应之兆 - Refers to auspicious signs or omens, often seen as divine messages or predictions.
  • 寳玺 - Refers to a precious seal or emblem, often with symbolic importance in imperial or religious contexts.
  • 皇元圣天子 - Refers to the great and holy emperor, a term used to describe a revered ruler.
  • 六合一统 - Refers to the unification of the six directions (north, south, east, west, up, and down), symbolizing imperial unity and power.
  • 宫车晚出 - Refers to the late appearance of the imperial chariot, symbolizing the emperor's presence or intervention.
  • 青宫 - Refers to the eastern palace, often associated with the heir to the throne.
  • 许慎説文 - Refers to Xu Shen's *Shuowen Jiezi*, a classic Chinese dictionary and etymological text.
  • 三代 - Refers to the Three Dynasties (Xia, Shang, and Zhou), a period of ancient Chinese history.

Proper Nouns

  • 燕 - The name of a region or state in ancient China.
  • 许慎 - The name of the author of *Shuowen Jiezi*.
  • 説文 - The title of Xu Shen's *Shuowen Jiezi*.
  • 玺 - The character for 'seal' or 'emblem.'
  • 玉 - The character for 'jade.'
  • 印 - The character for 'stamp' or 'official seal.'
  • 尔 - The character for 'you.'
  • 土 - The character for 'earth' or 'territory.'
  • 周 - The name of the Zhou Dynasty.
  • 太史籀 - The name of the Zhou historian who changed the character for 'seal.'
  • 三代 - The collective term for the Xia, Shang, and Zhou Dynasties.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 史籍并寳玺 - Combined records of history and the imperial seal, symbolizing imperial authority.
  • 文圗説 - Comprehensive account including written descriptions, images, and explanations.
  • 传国玺 - Imperial Seal of the Nation, symbol of imperial power and legitimacy.
  • 方四寸 - Literally 'square four inches,' describing the size of the seal.
  • 楚以卞和所献之璞琢而成璧 - Historical anecdote about the famous jade, the 'Heavenly Stone,' presented by Bi Gan to the state of Chu.
  • 后求昬于赵以纳聘焉 - Ambiguous phrase likely referring to the use of the jade in a marriage alliance with the state of Zhao.
  • 秦昭王请以十城易之而不获 - King of Qin's attempt to acquire the jade by exchanging ten cities, but he failed.
  • 命李斯其文 - Ordered Li Si to engrave the text on the seal, emphasizing the role of calligraphy in imperial symbols.
  • 玉工孙夀刻之 - Jade craftsman Sun Shou carved the seal.
  • 太平御览又以为蓝田玉所刻 - Alternative account suggesting the seal was made from Lantian jade.
  • 高祖即位服其玺 - Founding emperor wore the seal upon ascending the throne.
  • 因世传之谓为传国玺 - Seal passed down through generations and called the Imperial Seal of the Nation.
  • 孺子未立藏于长乐宫 - Young heir not yet established, seal hidden in the Changle Palace.
  • 及莽簒位 - Transition to Wang Mang's usurpation of the throne.
  • 使安阳侯王舜廹太后求之 - Wang Mang's actions to obtain the seal from the empress dowager.
  • 太后怒骂而不与 - Empress dowager angrily refused to give the seal.
  • 出玺投之地 - Empress dowager threw the seal to the ground, symbolizing her refusal.
  • 玺因归莽 - Seal returned to Wang Mang.
  • 及更始灭莽 - Overthrow of Wang Mang by Gengshi.
  • 校尉公賔得玺诣宛献于更始 - Military officer Gong Bin obtained and presented the seal to Gengshi.
  • 赤眉杀更始立盆子 - Red Eyebrows killed Gengshi and installed Liu Penzi as emperor.
  • 玺为盆子所有 - Seal in the possession of Liu Penzi.
  • 后盆子面缚奉玺于光武 - Liu Penzi surrendered the seal to Emperor Guangwu with hands bound behind his back.
  • 至献帝 - Until Emperor Xian, the last emperor of the Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 董卓作乱 - Dong Zhuo rebelled and seized control of the Han court.
  • 掌玺者投于井中 - Person in charge of the seal threw it into a well during the chaotic period.
  • 孙坚征董卓于井中得之 - Sun Jian, fighting against Dong Zhuo, found the seal in the well.
  • 术夺于坚妻 - Xiao Chao (a nickname for Yuan Shu) took the seal from Sun Jian's wife.
  • 术死荆州 - Yuan Shu died in Jingzhou.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 至宋哲宗咸阳民叚义献玉玺 - During the reign of Emperor Zhezong of Song, a man named Duan Yi from Xianyang presented a jade seal.
  • 徽宗为金所执 - Emperor Huizong was taken captive by the Jin.
  • 凡有寳玺金皆取之内玺一十有四 - All the jade seals were taken, including fourteen inner seals.
  • 青玉传国玺一其色与今所献玉玺相同 - One of them was a blue jade imperial seal, its color matching the one presented today.
  • 则知宋之南迁二百年无此寳玺也明矣 - Thus, it is clear that during the two hundred years of the Southern Song, this seal did not exist.
  • 然自金既取于宋之后寳玺出处得失亦未见明説 - However, after the Jin took it from the Song, the whereabouts and loss of the seal were not clearly documented.
  • 以及我元适集皇太孙寳命所归之际应期而出 - And during the time when my Yuan dynasty was gathering the imperial mandate and the fate of the crown prince, the seal appeared as if by destiny.
  • 臣职总御史亲会盛事不可以不録 - As a censor, I personally witnessed this grand event and cannot fail to record it.
  • 又圗中别有玺其文亦八旁注曰此传国玺背文也 - In the diagram, there is another seal with eight characters on the back, noted as the text on the back of the imperial seal.
  • 今见寳玺之背皆刻螭形蟠屈凹凸不齐徧视压四际无地可置此文 - Now, the back of the seal is carved with a coiling dragon, uneven and irregular, pressing against the four corners, leaving no space for the text.
  • 按太平御览太元十九年雝州刺史郄恢表慕容永称藩奉玺方六寸厚七分蟠螭为鼻今高四寸六分四边文下有字曰受天之命皇帝夀昌 - According to *T'ai-pai Yu-lan*, in the nineteenth year of Taiyuan, the governor of Yongzhou, Xie Hui, presented a seal to Murong Yong, who submitted to the Jin. The seal was six inches square and seven-tenths of an inch thick, with a coiling dragon as the nose. Now, it is four inches six-tenths of an inch high, with tortoise patterns on the four sides, and the inscription reads 'Received the mandate of heaven, Emperor's longevity.'
  • 原其所由未详厥始以斯言之当别是 - The origin is not detailed, but it can be inferred that it is different.

  • 金 - The Jin dynasty, a nomadic empire that conquered the Northern Song.

  • 慕容永 - A ruler of the Former Yan state during the Sixteen Kingdoms period.
  • 蟠螭 - A coiling dragon, often used in ancient Chinese art and symbolism.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一玺非今传国玺也 - This seal is not the current imperial seal.
  • 此又不可不辨 - This fact cannot be overlooked.
  • 臣彧诚惶诚恐顿首顿首谨奉牋上进以闻伏希听览微臣不胜瞻望之至谨言 - A formal expression of humility and submission, typical of official documents in classical Chinese.
  • 由儒学教授仕至国子司业卒 - Through Confucian studies, he became a teacher and served as a vice-principal of the Imperial Academy before his death.
  • 授汉中廉访司佥事仕至湖广廉访使卒 - He was appointed as a deputy of the Censorate of Hanzhong and later served as a censor of Huguang before his death.
  • 国史于安札尔传谓实廸乃国王缫古察之子 - The national history in the biography of Anzal states that he was the son of King Soguc.
  • 谓桓辩其文曰受命于天既夀永昌 - Huan explained the inscription as 'Granted by Heaven, long-lived and prosperous.'
  • 谓桓辩其文曰受天之命既夀永昌 - Huan explained the inscription as 'Receiving the mandate of Heaven, long-lived and prosperous.'
  • 盖秦别有受天之命皇帝夀昌一玺又非此玺 - There was another seal from the Qin dynasty with the inscription 'Receiving the mandate of Heaven, emperor long-lived and prosperous,' which is not this seal.
  • 今取宋薛尚功此编歴代钟鼎彛器欵识法帖碑本第十八卷内玺文模勒于后以备愽古者之一览云 - Now, taking from Song Dynasty's Xue Shanggong's compilation of inscriptions on bronze vessels, bells, ritual vessels, steles, and rubbings, the text of the seal from the eighteenth volume is reproduced here for the reference of scholars of ancient times.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 湖广廉访使 - Censor of Huguang, a high-ranking official in the Huguang region.
  • 实廸乃国王缫古察 - King Soguc, a historical figure mentioned in the national history.
  • 秦别有受天之命皇帝夀昌一玺 - A seal from the Qin dynasty with a specific inscription, indicating the continuity of imperial symbols.
  • 宋薛尚功 - Xue Shanggong, a Song Dynasty scholar known for his work on inscriptions.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 尚功云 - Title or phrase, possibly referring to a work or commentary.
  • 二玺文本只一器 - Means 'the two seal texts are actually one vessel,' suggesting the two versions of the text are essentially the same.
  • 縁传摹 - Refers to the process of copying or transmitting.
  • 字画不同 - Means 'the characters or calligraphy are different.'
  • 形制大小有异 - Means 'the shape and size differ.'
  • 因并刻之 - Means 'therefore, they were both engraved.'
  • 亦疑以传疑之意也 - Suggests that the engraving was done to preserve uncertainty or ambiguity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 瑞应泉 - Refers to a spring considered auspicious or blessed. Describes the spring's history, including its use in the production of tea during the Tang dynasty and its subsequent renaming during the Yuan dynasty.
  • 湖州长兴州金沙泉 - Refers to the location of the spring in the Zhejiang province, specifically in Changxing County. '金沙泉' is the name of the spring.
  • 唐时用此水造紫笋茶进贡 - Refers to the practice of using the spring water to make 'Zi Sun' tea, which was presented as a tribute to the emperor during the Tang dynasty.
  • 宋季屡加浚治泉迄不出 - '宋季' refers to the late Song dynasty. '屡加浚治' means 'repeatedly dredged and treated,' and '泉迄不出' means 'the spring still did not emerge.'
  • 至元十五年 - Refers to the 15th year of the Zhiyuan era of the Yuan dynasty.
  • 中书省遣官致祭 - Means 'the Central Secretariat sent officials to offer sacrifices.'
  • 疑 - Refers to the 72 tombs of Cao Cao, which are said to be filled with traps and uncertainties. Includes a poem by Yu Yingfu that expresses skepticism about the existence of a tomb containing Cao Cao's body.
  • 曹操疑七十二 - Refers to the legend of Cao Cao having 72 tombs, each designed to confuse and deter grave robbers.
  • 俞应符有诗题之曰 - '俞应符' is the name of the poet who wrote the poem. '有诗题之曰' means 'wrote a poem titled 'On the Doubtful Tombs.'
  • 生前欺天绝汉统 - Suggests that Cao Cao was deceitful and betrayed the Han dynasty.
  • 人生用智死即休 - Suggests that intelligence and cunning are only useful in life, and once dead, they are no longer needed.
  • 何有余机到丘垄 - Suggests that there is no more room for trickery in the grave.
  • 人言疑我不疑 - Expresses the poet's skepticism about the existence of a tomb containing Cao Cao's body.
  • 我有一法君未知 - Suggests that the poet has a method or knowledge that others do not.
  • 必有一藏君尸 - Suggests that one of the tombs must contain Cao Cao's body.
